ui.title_today: Is it Hot in Slovenia Today?
ui.subtitle_today: compared to the same date historically
ui.label_effects: Some effects of the climate changes

today.explain1: Today's peak is the highest temperature forecast across all 20 stations, ranked against 70+ years of ERA5-Land records for this same ±7-day window.
today.climate_context: Slovenia sits at the crossroads of four climate zones — Alpine, Mediterranean, Continental, and Pannonian — compressed into one of Europe's most climatically diverse countries. Central Europe is warming roughly 1.5× the global average. Temperatures have risen by nearly 2°C over the past 70 years. Spring arrives earlier in the Alps, Mediterranean droughts extend further inland, and the Pannonian northeast faces increasingly extreme heat. What felt like a typical summer in 1980 now ranks in the cooler half of recent decades.
today.chart_title: Slovenia daily max temperatures for the two weeks around {day_label} since {year_min}
today.trend_title: Slovenia peak temperatures around {day_label} · {year_min}–{year_max} · trend with projection to 2050
today.explain2: The curve shows how often each peak temperature occurred on days like today across all years. Colours mark climatological zones — from cold blue through the typical beige band to extreme red — so you can see at a glance where today sits.
today.explain3: Each dot is the 90th percentile of the national mean daily-maximum temperature across all stations in a ±30-day window around this date, for every year since {year_min}. The Theil-Sen trend is {trend} °C/decade ({sig}). At this rate, by 2050 the trend projects to {proj2050} °C. The shaded band is the 95% confidence interval on the slope. Note: this chart uses the national mean across all stations; the 'Is it hot today?' card above uses the national peak (single hottest station) — both are valid, but they measure different things.
today.trend_foot: Theil-Sen + TFPW MK: {trend} °C/decade · {sig} · τ = {tau} · 95% CI · {n_years} yrs

freezing.name: Freezing
freezing.desc: Among the coldest {d}s in our {record_years}-year record.

nope.name: Normal
nope.desc: Right around what {d} usually feels like in {country}.

hot.name: Hot
hot.desc: Among the hottest {d}s in our record.

hell.name: Hell
hell.desc: Exceptional heat — top 5% of all {d}s since {year_min}.

hero.verdict_warming: A century to warm by {sign}{t100} {unit} – at current pace, every {yrs} years adds another degree.
hero.verdict_cooling: A century to cool by {sign}{t100} {unit} – at current pace, every {yrs} years adds another degree.
hero.verdict_none: No detectable trend at this day of year.
hero.explain_reg: Each dot is one year's value; the rising line is the Theil-Sen trend — robust to outliers. The shaded band is its 95% confidence interval. Notice how the cluster of dots shifts upward over the decades: what counts as a normal year for this location keeps getting redefined. A trend that looks modest in °C per decade translates to a fundamentally different baseline by mid-century — warmer springs, hotter summers, and shorter cold seasons than any historical record describes.
hero.explain_cal: Each bar is the warming or cooling trend for that week of the year, in °C per decade. Orange means warming; blue means cooling. The pattern across the year shows which seasons are changing fastest — in Slovenia, summer bars are typically highest in the continental northeast, while spring warming is most pronounced at Alpine stations. A calendar dominated by orange across all seasons signals year-round warming: shorter winters, earlier springs, and longer droughts.

Ljubljana.baseline: Capital city in the Ljubljana Basin, ringed by hills that trap cold air in winter. Continental climate with warm summers and cold winters, amplified by the urban heat island.
Ljubljana.moderate: Warming shortens the winter fog season but intensifies summer heat stress. Urban fabric traps heat, making nights increasingly warm across the metropolitan area.
Ljubljana.bad: Summer heat waves exceed 35°C for multiple consecutive days. Elderly mortality rises. The basin geometry traps heat; air conditioning demand spikes and overwhelms the grid.
Ljubljana.extreme: Prolonged heat emergencies become annual events. The Ljubljanica river runs low in summer, threatening water supply. Urban greenery buckles under severe drought stress.
Ljubljana.catastrophic: Prolonged summer heat waves exceed the city's cooling capacity. Flash floods from intensified Alpine precipitation events repeatedly threaten the low-lying basin.

Maribor.baseline: Second largest city, Drava river valley, northeastern Slovenia. Continental climate with hot summers. A major viticulture region — warming is already shifting grape harvest dates earlier.
Maribor.moderate: Extended growing seasons benefit wine in the short term, but increasing drought stress and late frost risk after early bud break begin to threaten yields.
Maribor.bad: Wine harvest dates shift 3–4 weeks earlier. Traditional Styrian varieties struggle with heat stress. Phytosanitary problems intensify significantly.
Maribor.extreme: Traditional Styrian viticulture varieties become economically unviable. Irrigation demand from the Drava conflicts with ecological flow requirements.
Maribor.catastrophic: Repeated severe drought years collapse the Styrian wine economy. Drava river low-flow events endanger the valley's water supply and riparian ecosystems.

Celje.baseline: Savinja river valley, historically prone to severe flooding. Continental climate. The enclosed valley creates temperature inversions and frost pockets in winter.
Celje.moderate: Warming reduces frost days but intensifies convective rainfall events that trigger the Savinja floods for which the city is already known.
Celje.bad: 100-year flood events recur within decades. Flood protection infrastructure built for historical return periods is systematically overwhelmed.
Celje.extreme: Combined summer drought and winter flood extremes destabilise Savinja valley agriculture and infrastructure simultaneously.
Celje.catastrophic: More frequent extreme flood events overwhelm ageing flood defences. Simultaneous drought-flood alternation destabilises the agricultural Savinja plain.

Kranj.baseline: At the foot of the Kamnik-Savinja Alps, Sava river valley, 387m. Alpine influence keeps summers relatively cool. Gateway to Triglav National Park.
Kranj.moderate: Alpine snowpack decline reduces Sava summer river flow, affecting water supply for downstream users including Ljubljana.
Kranj.bad: Sava river summer low flows become severe. Hydroelectric production at the Sava chain of power plants drops significantly in dry years.
Kranj.extreme: Glacial retreat in the Kamnik-Savinja Alps accelerates. Rockfalls from destabilising slopes threaten valley infrastructure.
Kranj.catastrophic: Loss of reliable snowpack-fed summer flow in the Sava threatens the hydroelectric backbone of Slovenia's electricity grid.

Koper.baseline: Slovenia's only sea port, Adriatic coast, 10m elevation. Mediterranean climate: mild winters, dry summers, Bora wind events. Sea surface temperatures directly influence local conditions.
Koper.moderate: Sea surface warming extends the swimming season. Drought risk increases through summer as the Mediterranean drying signal strengthens.
Koper.bad: Saltwater intrusion into coastal aquifers intensifies. Mediterranean invasive species establish in Slovenian coastal waters.
Koper.extreme: Storm surge events increase in frequency and severity. Coastal agricultural land faces salinisation from sea spray and groundwater intrusion.
Koper.catastrophic: Rising Adriatic sea levels threaten port infrastructure and coastal agriculture. Extreme Bora events intensify as temperature gradients between sea and land increase.

Novo_Mesto.baseline: Krka river valley, southeastern Slovenia, 220m. Continental with some Pannonian influence. Important fruit-growing region — apples, pears, and hops.
Novo_Mesto.moderate: Warming extends the frost-free growing season but increases summer drought stress on the Krka river catchment.
Novo_Mesto.bad: Krka river base flow drops significantly in summer. Hop production — a major regional crop — faces heat and drought stress requiring costly irrigation.
Novo_Mesto.extreme: Multi-year droughts deplete the Krka river and groundwater. The fruit and hop economy requires fundamental restructuring with expensive new irrigation infrastructure.
Novo_Mesto.catastrophic: Repeated drought years deplete Krka river base flow. The hop and fruit economy of the Dolenjska region faces fundamental restructuring.

Murska_Sobota.baseline: Northeastern Pannonian plain, 189m. The most continental station in Slovenia — hottest summers, coldest winters, least precipitation. Agriculturally intensive: corn, sunflowers, wheat.
Murska_Sobota.moderate: Already the most drought-exposed region in Slovenia. Heat stress days above 35°C increasing fastest here. Crop water deficit is already significant.
Murska_Sobota.bad: Corn and sunflower yields drop 20–30% in drought years. Summer extremes above 38°C become regular. Agricultural groundwater extraction accelerates depletion.
Murska_Sobota.extreme: Multi-year drought sequences collapse traditional rain-fed agriculture. Irrigation demand exceeds Mura river sustainable yield.
Murska_Sobota.catastrophic: The Pannonian agricultural economy faces collapse under sustained multi-year drought. Groundwater depletion accelerates as irrigation demand exceeds recharge.

Nova_Gorica.baseline: Western Slovenia, lower Soča valley, 94m. Mediterranean-Alpine transition. Warm and sunny with Bora wind events. Important wine region — Brda and Karst appellations.
Nova_Gorica.moderate: Warming accelerates the Mediterranean drying trend. Bora events may intensify. Wine quality shifts as growing degree days accumulate faster.
Nova_Gorica.bad: Summer drought makes traditional Karst viticulture increasingly dependent on irrigation. Soča river low flows in summer threaten ecology.
Nova_Gorica.extreme: The Soča's cold-water fishery — the iconic Marble Trout — faces thermal extinction in its lower reaches as summer temperatures exceed tolerance.
Nova_Gorica.catastrophic: Extreme summer drought makes traditional Karst and Brda viticulture unviable without irrigation. Soča river low flows threaten biodiversity and tourism.

Postojna.baseline: Kras plateau, 549m. Famous for temperature inversions — cold air pools in the karst depression making it one of the coldest spots in southern Slovenia despite its latitude.
Postojna.moderate: Warming weakens temperature inversions, reducing the extreme cold that defines Postojna's microclimate. Cave ecosystem temperatures begin to shift.
Postojna.bad: Cave interior temperatures — stable at 10°C for millennia — begin rising measurably. Karst spring discharge decreases in summer droughts.
Postojna.extreme: Postojna Cave's endemic fauna — the olm (Proteus anguinus) and cave beetles found nowhere else on Earth — face thermal and hydrological stress.
Postojna.catastrophic: Karst aquifer recharge falters under prolonged drought. Postojna Cave's endemic fauna faces thermal stress with no escape route.

Ptuj.baseline: Oldest continuously inhabited town in Slovenia, Drava river valley, 228m. Northeastern continental zone. Wine, hops, and grain agriculture.
Ptuj.moderate: Drava river low-flow events increase in frequency, affecting the cooling water supply for the Formin hydroelectric plant downstream.
Ptuj.bad: Wine harvest dates shift significantly earlier. The Drava valley's agricultural water demand increasingly conflicts with ecological flow requirements.
Ptuj.extreme: Drava river extreme low-flow years become regular. The Formin hydroelectric plant faces forced curtailment in drought years.
Ptuj.catastrophic: Compound drought and heat extremes destabilise the Drava valley agricultural economy. Flood-drought alternation damages riverside infrastructure repeatedly.

Velenje.baseline: Šalek valley, 405m, central Slovenia. Built around lignite coal mining — Slovenia's largest remaining mine. Highland continental microclimate influenced by the Šalek lakes.
Velenje.moderate: As coal mining phases out, climate impacts on the post-industrial landscape include increased flood risk in subsidence zones around the mines.
Velenje.bad: The Šalek lakes — formed by mining subsidence — face rising evaporation and algal blooms as water temperatures increase. Water management becomes complex.
Velenje.extreme: Post-coal transition is complicated by climate extremes. The planned renewable energy transition for the region faces land-use conflicts with climate adaptation needs.
Velenje.catastrophic: Water management in the subsidence lakes becomes critical as evaporation increases. The post-mining landscape is highly vulnerable to hydrological extremes.

Trbovlje.baseline: Zasavje region, Sava canyon, 230m. The narrowest inhabited valley in Slovenia — heavily industrialised coal and cement town. Valley confinement creates distinctive temperature inversions.
Trbovlje.moderate: Warming reduces winter inversions that currently trap air pollution, but increases summer heat stress in this enclosed, densely built environment.
Trbovlje.bad: Summer heat waves in the canyon are amplified by its geometry and industrial heat sources. The valley has limited green space for cooling, creating serious public health risks.
Trbovlje.extreme: Extreme precipitation events in the Sava catchment above Trbovlje increase catastrophic flood risk in the canyon. Industrial heritage buildings are vulnerable.
Trbovlje.catastrophic: Extreme heat events in the canyon are amplified by its geometry. Sava flood risk increases as Alpine precipitation intensifies.

Tolmin.baseline: Soča/Isonzo valley, 194m. The warmest valley in Slovenia despite its inland Alpine position — warm Mediterranean air penetrates far inland through the Soča corridor. Extreme precipitation events (up to 400mm/day) make this the wettest area in Europe.
Tolmin.moderate: Warming intensifies the already-extreme precipitation events that define the upper Soča catchment. The valley's exceptional biodiversity faces growing thermal pressure.
Tolmin.bad: Soča river summer flows drop significantly as precipitation timing shifts. The Marble Trout's cold-water habitat contracts northward and upward.
Tolmin.extreme: Extreme flood events on the Soča-Idrijca system recur more frequently. The Soča's emerald-green colour — dependent on glacial flour — diminishes as glaciers retreat.
Tolmin.catastrophic: Catastrophic flood events on the Soča become more frequent and severe. The cold-water fishery and nature tourism economy are fundamentally threatened.

Kocevje.baseline: Kočevski Rog region, 467m, covered by the largest primeval forest remnant in Central Europe. One of the coldest spots in lowland Slovenia. Bears, lynx, and wolves at Europe's highest density.
Kocevje.moderate: Warming shifts forest composition from cold-adapted fir-beech to more thermophilic species. Bark beetle outbreaks — already intensifying — accelerate under warmer, drier summers.
Kocevje.bad: The primeval forest faces structural transformation under compound drought and bark beetle pressure. Forest dieback opens gaps vulnerable to wind damage and fire.
Kocevje.extreme: Large-scale bark beetle-triggered forest dieback opens the primeval forest to fire risk previously negligible in this humid, cool environment.
Kocevje.catastrophic: The Kočevje primeval forest — a European biodiversity refuge — faces irreversible structural change. Large carnivore habitat contracts as forest cover declines.

Ilirska_Bistrica.baseline: Pivka basin, 440m. Transition zone between Mediterranean and continental climates. Cold winters with Bora wind, warm summers. The Pivka river disappears underground into the world's largest cave system.
Ilirska_Bistrica.moderate: Warming reduces winter snowpack that feeds the Pivka karst springs. The Bora wind pattern may shift, altering the thermal regime of this transitional zone.
Ilirska_Bistrica.bad: Pivka river surface flow disappears earlier in the season as karst spring flows decline. The unique hydrological cycle — surface river entering caves — is disrupted.
Ilirska_Bistrica.extreme: The Postojna-Planina Cave System, fed by the Pivka underground, faces reduced discharge and rising water temperatures, threatening cave fauna.
Ilirska_Bistrica.catastrophic: Karst spring flows feeding the Pivka river dry up in summer droughts. The unique hydrological system is disrupted with cascading effects on cave ecosystems downstream.

Domzale.baseline: Ljubljana basin, 301m, immediately northeast of the capital. Suburban and light industrial. Continental climate closely mirroring Ljubljana, with slightly less urban heat island effect.
Domzale.moderate: As the Ljubljana metropolitan area expands, urban heat island effects intensify. The flat basin is vulnerable to summer heat accumulation and winter fog.
Domzale.bad: Summer heat island across the Ljubljana basin creates persistent heat stress for the region's 400,000 residents. Energy demand for cooling strains the grid.
Domzale.extreme: Compound heat and drought events across the basin reduce quality of life and increase heat-related mortality significantly.
Domzale.catastrophic: Urbanisation combined with climate warming creates a persistent heat island across the Ljubljana basin, with compounding effects on energy demand and public health.

Ratece.baseline: Tarvisio basin, Julian Alps, 864m. The highest valley station and one of the driest spots in Slovenia despite Alpine location — in the rain shadow of the Karavanke. Extreme cold winters, heavy snowpack.
Ratece.moderate: Snowpack depth and duration are declining measurably. Ski season shortens from both ends. Spring runoff timing shifts earlier, affecting summer river flows throughout Slovenia.
Ratece.bad: Kranjska Gora ski resort faces economically unviable snow seasons. Multiple consecutive poor snow years force fundamental business model transformation.
Ratece.extreme: Permafrost thaw in the Julian Alps above Rateče triggers increasing rockfall and slope instability. Alpine infrastructure and hiking routes are at risk.
Ratece.catastrophic: Loss of reliable winter snowpack ends the ski economy of the Kranjska Gora valley. Summer alpine tourism disrupted by rapid glacial retreat and thawing permafrost.

Kredarica.baseline: Summit of the Triglav massif, 2514m — the highest weather station in Slovenia and one of the longest continuous high-altitude records in the Alps (since 1954). Permanent snow, glacial remnants, extreme winds.
Kredarica.moderate: The Triglav glacier — Slovenia's last — is retreating visibly year by year. Permafrost thaw destabilises the high ridges. Warming here is roughly twice the lowland rate.
Kredarica.bad: The Triglav glacier loses more than half its remaining volume. The iconic three-peaked silhouette on Slovenia's national coat of arms faces permanent alteration.
Kredarica.extreme: The Triglav glacier disappears entirely. High-alpine ecosystem above the treeline collapses. The headwaters of the Sava, Soča, and Sava Dolinka all originate in this massif.
Kredarica.catastrophic: Complete loss of the Triglav glacier within decades — a national symbol and source of Slovenia's major rivers. Alpine ecosystem collapse triggers cascading effects throughout the watershed.

about.heading: About us
about.col1_text: podnebje.si visualises 75+ years of ERA5-Land reanalysis data for 18 locations across Slovenia. Every trend, chart, and projection is computed from the same underlying dataset using open statistical methods. No chatbot. No ads. Just data.
about.col2_text1: Southeastern Europe is warming at nearly twice the global average rate. The effects are already here - longer summers, shrinking rivers, record-breaking heat, and fires burning wider every year.
about.col2_text2: Numbers make it harder to look away. When you see how many more hot days we've had this decade compared to the last, or how rainfall has shifted over time, it stops feeling like abstract news and starts feeling like what it is: a local problem, happening now. Understanding what's changing - here, in our own backyard - is the first step to doing something about it.
about.col3_text: Built on ERA5-Land reanalysis data from the Copernicus Climate Change Service. Statistical methods: Theil-Sen slope with Yue-Wang TFPW Mann-Kendall significance testing. SPEI computed via 3-parameter log-logistic distribution. Contact: info@podnebje.si

quotes.author: Sylvia Earle (Oceanographer)
quotes.quote: Even if you never have the chance to see or touch the ocean, the ocean touches you with every breath you take, every drop of water you drink, every bite you consume. Everyone everywhere is inextricably connected to and utterly dependent upon the existence of the sea.

quotes.author: Guy McPherson (Scientist)
quotes.quote: If you really think that the environment is less important than the economy, try holding your breath while you count your money.

quotes.author: Albert Einstein (Physicist)
quotes.quote: The world will not be destroyed by those who do evil, but by those who watch them without doing anything.

quotes.author: Tim Flannery (Scientist)
quotes.quote: One of the biggest obstacles to making a start on climate change is that it has become a cliche before it has even been understood.

quotes.author: Desmond Tutu (Archbishop)
quotes.quote: Twenty-five years ago, people could be excused for not knowing much, or doing much, about climate change. Today we have no excuse.

quotes.author: John Delaney (Politician)
quotes.quote: Climate change is the environmental challenge of this generation, and it is imperative that we act before it's too late.

quotes.author: Native American Proverb
quotes.quote: We do not inherit the earth from our ancestors. We borrow it from our children.

quotes.author: Jay Inslee (Politician)
quotes.quote: We are the first generation to feel the sting of climate change, and we are the last generation that can do something about it.

quotes.author: Bill Nye (Science Communicator)
quotes.quote: Climate change is happening, humans are causing it, and I think this is perhaps the most serious environmental issue facing us.

quotes.author: Frances Beinecke (Environmental Leader)
quotes.quote: Wind and other clean, renewable energy will help end our reliance on fossil fuels and combat the severe threat that climate change poses to humans and wildlife alike.

quotes.author: David Suzuki (Scientist)
quotes.quote: If we pollute the air, water and soil that keep us alive and well, and destroy the biodiversity that allows natural systems to function, no amount of money will save us.

quotes.author: Christian de Duve (Scientist)
quotes.quote: The cost of our success is the exhaustion of natural resources, leading to energy crises, climate change, pollution, and the destruction of our habitat.

quotes.author: Al Gore (Former U.S. Vice President)
quotes.quote: As human beings, we are vulnerable to confusing the unprecedented with the improbable - but the exceptions can kill you, and climate change is one of those exceptions.

quotes.author: Malcolm Turnbull (Politician)
quotes.quote: Climate change is a global problem - truly catastrophic consequences are likely to ensue.

quotes.author: David Attenborough (Naturalist)
quotes.quote: There is no question that climate change is happening; the only arguable point is what part humans are playing in it.

quotes.author: Barack Obama (Former U.S. President)
quotes.quote: The shift to a cleaner energy economy won't happen overnight. But the debate is settled. Climate change is a fact.

quotes.author: Jack Dangermond (Environmental Technologist)
quotes.quote: The world that you and I live in is increasingly challenged: climate change, loss of nature — these are not good stories.

quotes.author: Yuval Noah Harari (Historian)
quotes.quote: We are all living together on a single planet, and if you don't have some kind of global cooperation, you can't tackle climate change.

quotes.author: Nicholas Stern (Economist)
quotes.quote: Climate change is a result of the greatest market failure the world has seen.

quotes.author: Bill Gates (Business Leader)
quotes.quote: By the time we see that climate change is really bad, your ability to fix it is extremely limited.

quotes.author: Leonardo DiCaprio (Actor)
quotes.quote: I play fictitious characters often solving fictitious problems. I believe mankind has looked at climate change as if it were fiction.

quotes.author: Ernest Hemingway (Author)
quotes.quote: The Earth is a fine place and worth fighting for.

quotes.author: Terri Swearingen (Activist)
quotes.quote: We are living on this planet as if we had another one to go to.

quotes.author: Rob Stewart (Filmmaker)
quotes.quote: The Earth should not be a worse place after my life than it was when I was born here.

quotes.author: Pope Francis (Religious Leader)
quotes.quote: God gave us the earth, to till and to keep in a balanced and respectful way.

quotes.author: David King (Scientist)
quotes.quote: Climate change is the most severe problem that we are facing today, more serious even than the threat of terrorism.

quotes.author: Marco Lambertini (Conservation Leader)
quotes.quote: A planet being pushed to the edge will eventually turn on us.

quotes.author: Paul Krugman (Economist)
quotes.quote: The planet will continue to cook.

quotes.author: Arnold Schwarzenegger (Actor & Politician)
quotes.quote: The facts are there that we have created a self-inflicted wound through global warming.

quotes.author: Jonathon Porritt (Environmentalist)
quotes.quote: The future will be green or not at all.

quotes.author: David Attenborough (Naturalist)
quotes.quote: Dealing with global warming means that we have to stop waste.

quotes.author: Al Gore (Former U.S. Vice President)
quotes.quote: We are running out of time, and we must have a planetary solution to a planetary crisis.

quotes.author: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (U.S. Politician)
quotes.quote: We don't have time to sit on our hands as our planet burns. It's life or death.

quotes.author: Archana Soreng (Climate Advocate)
quotes.quote: Indigenous people, local communities, young people, women and girls, should be leaders of climate action and not victims of climate policies.

quotes.author: Richard Branson (Business Leader)
quotes.quote: Climate change is a huge challenge, but it can be brought in line if governments, businesses and individuals work together.

quotes.author: Leah Thomas (Environmental Communicator)
quotes.quote: We can't save the planet without uplifting the voices of its people, especially those most often unheard.

quotes.author: John Lewis (U.S. Civil Rights Leader)
quotes.quote: It is our responsibility to leave this planet cleaner and greener. That must be our legacy.

quotes.author: Greta Thunberg (Climate Activist)
quotes.quote: We can no longer let the people in power decide what hope is. Hope is telling the truth. Hope is taking action.

quotes.author: Mark Ruffalo (Actor)
quotes.quote: Climate change is the greatest threat to our existence in our short history on this planet. Nobody's going to buy their way out of its effects.

quotes.author: Tom Steyer (Investor)
quotes.quote: Demanding that our leaders take action on climate change is safeguarding our health and protecting the future of our children.

quotes.author: Tony Blair (Former UK Prime Minister)
quotes.quote: Global warming is too serious for the world any longer to ignore its danger.

quotes.author: Leonardo DiCaprio (Actor & Environmentalist)
quotes.quote: Climate change is real. It is happening right now. We need to work collectively together and stop procrastinating.

quotes.author: Gisele Bundchen (UN Goodwill Ambassador)
quotes.quote: Deforestation leads to climate change — it's a vicious cycle.

quotes.author: James Cameron (Film Director)
quotes.quote: It's important for me, for my kids, that we're not going to leave them in a world that's in shambles.

quotes.author: Elizabeth May (Politician)
quotes.quote: I hold a vision of this blue green planet, safe and in balance.

quotes.author: Barack Obama (Former U.S. President)
quotes.quote: The United States not only recognises our role in creating this problem, we embrace our responsibility to do something about it.

quotes.author: Xi Jinping (Chinese President)
quotes.quote: Tackling climate change is a shared mission for mankind.

quotes.author: Pope Francis (Religious Leader)
quotes.quote: Now or never. Every year the problems are getting worse.

quotes.author: Edi Rama (Prime Minister, Albania)
quotes.quote: Life goes on with its old habits and our speeches about fighting climate change change nothing.

quotes.author: Wopke Hoekstra (European Commissioner for Climate)
quotes.quote: Climate neutrality is not just a slogan, it is also our commitment under the Paris Agreement.

quotes.author: Simon Stiell (UN Climate Change Executive Secretary)
quotes.quote: The climate crisis is an urgent national security crisis that should be at the top of every cabinet room agenda.

quotes.author: Simon Stiell (UN Climate Change Executive Secretary)
quotes.quote: Surrender is not an option. And half measures are a recipe for failure.

quotes.author: Carl Sagan (Physicist)
quotes.quote: Anything else you're interested in is not going to happen if you can't breathe the air and drink the water.

quotes.author: Michio Kaku (Physicist)
quotes.quote: Global warming is real. It is a slow-moving catastrophe that will reshape the geography of the planet.

quotes.author: Robert Redford (Actor)
quotes.quote: The environment should be in the category of national security. Defense of our resources is just as important as defense abroad.

quotes.author: Cate Blanchett (Actor)
quotes.quote: Climate change is a tragedy that is unfolding before our eyes, yet we still look away.

quotes.author: Dr. Lučka Kajfež Bogataj (Climate scientist, University of Ljubljana)
quotes.quote: The Triglav glacier is not just a symbol — it is a measurement. Its disappearance is the most honest data point we have.

quotes.author: Dr. Lučka Kajfež Bogataj (Climate scientist, University of Ljubljana)
quotes.quote: V Sloveniji se povprečna temperatura v zadnjem stoletju dvignila za skoraj 2 stopinji Celzija. To niso samo številke.

quotes.author: Anonymous Alpine guide (Triglav National Park)
quotes.quote: The mountains do not lie. Every retreating snowline is a sentence in a story we are still writing.

quotes.author: Bora Dugic (Musician)
quotes.quote: Nature has its own harmony, and when humanity alters the climate, we introduce a devastating discord into the world.

quotes.author: Janis Varoufakis (Economist/Politician)
quotes.quote: Climate change is the ultimate market failure; capitalism is destroying the ecological foundations upon which it relies.

quotes.author: Dusan Kovacevic (Playwright)
quotes.quote: We are writing a tragic final act for the planet, pretending that the script can be changed at the last second.

quotes.author: Stephen Hawking (Physicist)
quotes.quote: We are close to the tipping point where global warming becomes irreversible.

quotes.author: Richard Feynman (Physicist)
quotes.quote: For a successful technology, reality must take precedence over public relations, for nature cannot be fooled.

quotes.author: Olafur Eliasson (Artist)
quotes.quote: Climate change is not an abstract concept; it is a physical reality that demands a creative response.

quotes.author: Jane Fonda (Actor)
quotes.quote: This is a collective crisis that requires collective action. The climate crisis is an all-hands-on-deck emergency.

quotes.author: Barack Obama (Politician)
quotes.quote: We are the first generation to feel the effect of climate change and the last generation who can do something about it.

quotes.author: Greta Thunberg (Activist)
quotes.quote: Our house is on fire. I am here to say, our house is on fire.

quotes.author: Marina Abramovic (Performance Artist)
quotes.quote: Adding climate change, it truly feels like the end of the world because we are destroying the planet.

quotes.author: Slavoj Zizek (Philosopher)
quotes.quote: We like to be guilty since, if we are guilty, it all depends on us. We engage in frantic activities just so we can be sure that we are doing something.

quotes.author: Barack Obama (44th President of the United States)
quotes.quote: We are the first generation to feel the effect of climate change and the last generation that can do something about it.

quotes.author: Mahatma Gandhi
quotes.quote: What we are doing to the forests of the world is but a mirror reflection of what we are doing to ourselves and to one another.

quotes.author: Paul McCartney (Musician)
quotes.quote: The person in the song will be symbolic of politicians who argue that climate change is a hoax. This idea of 'It's all gonna be fine, don't worry.' Oh yeah, sure, there are icebergs melting but it doesn't matter.

quotes.author: Billie Eilish (Musician)
quotes.quote: Hills burn in California. My turn to ignore ya. Don't say I didn't warn ya.

quotes.author: Neil Young (Musician)
quotes.quote: We heard the warning calls. Ignored them. We watched the weather change. We saw the fires and floods. We saw the people rise divided. We fought each other while we lost our coveted prize.

quotes.author: Lana Del Rey (Musician)
quotes.quote: L.A.'s in flames, it's getting hot. The world is getting hotter. Hope is a dwindling resource. We don't have much time left.

quotes.author: Pharrell Williams (Musician)
quotes.quote: We have to take care of the planet. We have one shot at this. We need to make eco-friendly choices and support sustainable fashion.

quotes.author: Eddie Vedder (Musician)
quotes.quote: We need to minimize our carbon footprint and raise awareness about global warming. Every action counts.

quotes.author: Dave Matthews (Musician)
quotes.quote: The environment is a shared responsibility. We must support organizations fighting for our planet's future.

quotes.author: Xiuhtezcatl Martinez (Musician & Activist)
quotes.quote: How will you look your child in the eyes and tell them their future wasn't worth fighting for, could've done more but didn't listen, didn't wake up, didn't speak up, didn't fight back when there was still time.

quotes.author: Jaden Smith (Actor & Musician)
quotes.quote: We have to save our planet. It's the only one we have.

quotes.author: Emma Thompson (Actor)
quotes.quote: We need urgent action on the climate crisis. There is no Planet B.

quotes.author: Julia Louis-Dreyfus (Actor)
quotes.quote: Corporate interests have sought to mislead the public about climate change. We must fight for the truth.

quotes.author: Shailene Woodley (Actor)
quotes.quote: We have to preserve natural resources for future generations. The time for action is now.

quotes.author: Cameron Diaz (Actor)
quotes.quote: Environmental issues from rainforest deforestation to water pollution affect us all. We must act responsibly.

quotes.author: Gwyneth Paltrow (Actor)
quotes.quote: We can make better choices that benefit our health and the environment. Every small step counts.

quotes.author: Matt Damon (Actor)
quotes.quote: Climate justice is essential. Clean water and a healthy environment are basic human rights.

quotes.author: Emma Watson (Actor)
quotes.quote: As consumers we have so much power to change the world by just being careful in what we buy.

quotes.author: Jane Goodall (Primatologist)
quotes.quote: Fortunately, nature is amazingly resilient: places we have destroyed, given time and help, can once again support life, and endangered species can be given a second chance.

quotes.author: Al Gore (Politician)
quotes.quote: Solutions to the climate crisis are within reach, but in order to capture them, we must take urgent action today across every level of society.

quotes.author: Joaquin Phoenix (Actor)
quotes.quote: We feel entitled to artificially inseminate a cow and steal her baby, even though her cries of anguish are unmistakable. We think we need to sacrifice something to change. But we can be inventive.

quotes.author: Harrison Ford (Actor)
quotes.quote: Nature doesn't need people. People need nature. Nature would survive the extinction of the human being and go on just fine, but human culture, human beings, cannot survive without nature.

quotes.author: Woody Harrelson (Actor)
quotes.quote: We have to start thinking about the planet as our home, not as something to conquer. We need to work with nature, not against it.

quotes.author: Meryl Streep (Actor)
quotes.quote: Climate change is real. We owe it to future generations to acknowledge the science and take action.

quotes.author: Jason Momoa (Actor)
quotes.quote: The ocean is dying. We need to do everything we can to protect our waters and our planet for our children.

quotes.author: Bono (Musician)
quotes.quote: Climate change is not just an environmental issue. It's a human rights issue, an economic issue, and a security issue.

quotes.author: Chris Martin — Coldplay (Musician)
quotes.quote: We've decided to put the tour on pause until we work out how to make it sustainable and beneficial for the environment.

quotes.author: Jack Johnson (Musician)
quotes.quote: We all have a responsibility to protect the planet. Small changes in our daily lives can make a big difference.

quotes.author: Sting (Musician)
quotes.quote: We are destroying our planet with such reckless abandon. The rainforests are the lungs of the world and we're cutting them down.

effects: Hotter and longer summers with more frequent extreme heatwaves
Record-breaking temperatures becoming more common each decade
Urban heat islands intensifying in cities like Athens, Belgrade, and Bucharest
Warmer winters with fewer frost days
Increased number of tropical nights (temperatures staying above 20°C at night)
Heat-related deaths rising, especially among elderly populations
Animals and plants shifting their seasonal behaviors (earlier springs, later autumns)
Severe and prolonged droughts reducing water availability
Decreased summer rainfall with more erratic precipitation patterns
Lower river flows threatening drinking water supplies
Shrinking lakes and reservoirs across the region
Groundwater depletion accelerating due to over-extraction and low recharge
Reduced snowpack on Balkan mountain ranges
Glacial and permafrost retreat in higher elevations
Longer dry seasons stressing both ecosystems and agriculture
Increased competition over shared water resources between countries
More intense flash floods during autumn and spring
Stronger and more unpredictable storms
Increased frequency of hail events damaging crops and property
More frequent extreme rainfall events causing urban flooding
River flooding becoming more severe in short, intense episodes
Landslides increasing due to unstable, saturated soils after heavy rain
Crop yield losses due to heat stress and drought
Wine-growing regions shifting northward and to higher altitudes
Olive cultivation becoming viable further north but declining in the south
Soil degradation and desertification spreading in southern areas
Longer and more intense pest and disease seasons harming crops
Pollinator decline linked to climate disruption affecting harvests
Livestock suffering from heat stress, reducing productivity
Irrigation water demands rising sharply, straining resources
More frequent and destructive wildfires, especially in the Mediterranean and Alpine foothills
Forest die-offs due to drought and bark beetle outbreaks
Invasive plant species spreading into new territories
Fish populations in rivers declining due to warmer, lower water levels
Coral bleaching and habitat loss in the Adriatic and Aegean seas
Migratory bird patterns shifting, with some species no longer wintering in the region
Spread of invasive species disrupting local ecosystems
Sea level rise threatening low-lying coastal areas along the Adriatic and Black Sea
Coastal erosion accelerating, destroying beaches and infrastructure
Mediterranean Sea warming faster than the global ocean average
Increased frequency of jellyfish blooms disrupting tourism and fisheries
Saltwater intrusion into coastal freshwater aquifers
Spread of tropical diseases such as West Nile virus and dengue fever
Expansion of tiger mosquito and sandfly habitats carrying new pathogens
Worsening air quality due to heat, drought, and wildfires
Increased respiratory and cardiovascular illness from heat and pollution
Mental health impacts from climate anxiety and repeated disaster exposure
Climate-driven migration from rural and coastal areas into cities
Economic losses in tourism due to extreme heat and degraded natural environments
Strain on energy grids from soaring summer air-conditioning demand
Higher food prices driven by regional crop failures
Increased dust storms and Saharan dust intrusions reducing air quality

climate_risks.heading: Impact risk:
climate_risks.Ljubljana: Urban heat island — summer heat stress and flash flood risk in the Ljubljana Basin
climate_risks.Maribor: Drought stress on viticulture — earlier harvests and shifting grape varieties
climate_risks.Celje: Savinja flood intensification combined with increasing summer drought
climate_risks.Kranj: Alpine snowpack loss reducing Sava river summer flow
climate_risks.Koper: Mediterranean drought extension — saltwater intrusion and storm surge risk
climate_risks.Novo_Mesto: Krka river drought threatening hop and fruit agriculture
climate_risks.Murska_Sobota: Pannonian heat and drought — most extreme temperature swings in Slovenia
climate_risks.Nova_Gorica: Mediterranean drying — Soča cold-water ecosystem and Karst viticulture at risk
climate_risks.Postojna: Karst aquifer drought — cave ecosystem and endemic fauna under thermal stress
climate_risks.Ptuj: Drava low flows — viticulture water stress and hydropower curtailment
climate_risks.Velenje: Post-mining water management under intensifying drought and flood extremes
climate_risks.Trbovlje: Sava canyon heat amplification and increasing flood risk
climate_risks.Tolmin: Soča extreme flood intensification — cold-water ecosystem contraction
climate_risks.Kocevje: Bark beetle-driven primeval forest dieback — large carnivore habitat loss
climate_risks.Ilirska_Bistrica: Karst spring depletion — Pivka underground river system disruption
climate_risks.Domzale: Urban basin heat island — compound heat and drought for the Ljubljana region
climate_risks.Ratece: Alpine snowpack loss — ski economy threat and permafrost slope instability
climate_risks.Kredarica: Triglav glacier loss — headwater desiccation for Slovenia's major rivers

welcome.body1: Welcome to Slovenia Climate Explorer — 75 years of ERA5-Land climate data for 18 stations across Slovenia, from the Adriatic coast to the Triglav summit.
welcome.body2: Explore warming trends, seasonal patterns, drought indices, and where today's temperature sits in its historical context.

what_can_we_do.heading: What can we do?
s1_items: The Šoštanj thermal power plant (TEŠ 6) is Slovenia's single largest source of CO₂. Its planned closure by 2033 is critical — supporting an accelerated timeline matters.
The Krško nuclear plant (NEK) produces ~40% of Slovenia's electricity with zero CO₂. Its continued operation and eventual replacement is the backbone of decarbonisation.
Slovenia has excellent solar potential, especially in the southwest. Eko sklad subsidies cover rooftop solar, heat pumps, and insulation — visit ekosklad.si to see what applies to your home.
s2_items: Use the train. Ljubljana–Maribor and Ljubljana–Koper are competitive with driving in time and far better in emissions. The rail network is underused.
For short urban trips, cycling infrastructure is expanding in Ljubljana, Maribor, and Koper — infrastructure only improves when people use it.
s3_items: Slovenia's forests sequester roughly 3 million tonnes of CO₂ per year. Buy certified (FSC/PEFC) wood products and support forest protection legislation.
The Kočevje primeval forest is one of Europe's last large wilderness areas. It needs permanent legal protection — contact your MP.
s4_items: Ask your občina (municipality) for its climate adaptation plan. Flood preparedness, urban tree planting, and heat emergency protocols should be standard — if they are not, that is a question worth asking.
s5_items: Eko sklad (ekosklad.si) — green finance and subsidies for households
